CNNs trained for object recognition heavily rely on texture information, leading to misclassification.
Human visual systems rely primarily on shape and structures over texture for object recognition, exhibiting a strong 'shape bias.'
The shape bias in human vision systems leads to prioritizing shape and structures over texture for object recognition and categorization.

The shape bias of human subjects is compared with transformer-based networks and sparse CNNs in terms of frontier boundary. The study explores the influence of sparsity in biological neural networks on inducing shape bias in neural networks.

The sparsity in biological neural networks is hypothesized to induce shape bias in neuronal representation. The study imposed a sparsity mechanism to test this hypothesis and found that it indeed introduced more shape bias in the network.

The study aims to explore whether the constraint of high degree of strong-response sparsity in biological neural networks can induce shape bias in neural networks. The sparsity mechanism introduced indeed induced more shape bias in the network.

The Sparse Top-K With Mean Replacement is an alternative scheme to determine the relative importance of the Top-K values or positions in the Top-K operation.
The Top-K selection operation involves replacing all the Top-K responses in a channel with the mean of the Top-K responses in that channel, as defined by Top_K_Mean_Rpl.
The Top_K_Mean_Rpl operation has an impact on the object performance in an object recognition network, as discussed in Section 4.3.

The dataset defines both shape bias and texture bias. Shape bias is the number of correct shape recognitions divided by the number of correct recognitions, while texture bias is the number of correct texture recognitions divided by the number of correct recognitions.

Values of Top-K responses compressed to the mean responses of each channel
The Top-K responses matter by compressing the information in each channel to the mean responses of the Top-K responses of that channel.
The effective compression reduces the communication rate by 3 orders of magnitude.
The information of each channel is reduced to only a binary mask indicating the Top-K responding locations.
The information of each channel is also reduced to a single float number that relays the channel’s weighted contribution to downstream layers.
Replacing the Top-K values with the mean results in enormous data compression.
The spatial map of the Top-K activation is suggested to be more important than the precise values of the Top-K responses.
A significant amount of the object shapes features are actually encoded in the occupancy map of significant neural activities.

The synthesis quality measurements for adding the Top-K operation to the FastGAN network show consistent improvement in terms of FID and KID scores.
The Top-K operation leads to the generation of objects (e.g. the jeep) that are more structurally coherent and distinct compared to the original FastGAN.
A 21.1% improvement on FID scores and 50.8% improvement on KID scores is observed for the Jeep-100 class when 5% sparsity was imposed during training.

When 5% sparsity is imposed on Train-100 dataset, the FID is increased by 12% and the KID performance is boosted by 33.4%.

By applying sparse constraint using Top-K, each channel effectively performs binary spatial segmentation
Even though Top-K Operation is not fully differentiable, the network is able to relocate the spatial activation mass smoothly towards connected meaningful parts
CNN activations are locally smooth, i.e. two adjacent pixels in layer Ln are linked by the amount of overlap between their corresponding input patches in Ln−1.
Top-K operation increases the responsibility of each individual neuron to the loss function.

When neurons i and j are being selected as Top-K, their responses are likely similar to each other. However, if their corresponding spatial location in the output has different semantic meanings, they will receive diverse gradients which will be then amplified by the increased individual responsibility.

The diverging gradients would lead to the value difference of two neuron i and j’s gradients, resulting in one of them leaving the Top-K group while only the semantic similar ones remaining in the Top-K sets.
This might suggest a principle we call neurons fire together, optimize together during CNN Top-K training, which could lead to the observed emerging of semantic parts and subparts.
This localist code could further connect to recognition by component theory which constrains the representation by semantic parts segmentation.

The observation that sparse coding operation can induce shape bias in deep learning networks suggests sparsity might also contribute to shape bias in human visual systems.

This study investigates whether the sparse coding motif in neuroscience can induce shape bias in deep learning networks, with results suggesting that sparsity might also contribute to shape bias in human visual systems.
The sparse coding operation in deep learning networks may contribute to shape bias in human visual systems.
